name: Naraka
description: Naraka, in various Dharmic religions including Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism, and Sikhism, represents a realm of suffering, often translated as 'hell' or 'purgatory.' It is not a permanent, eternal damnation in the Abrahamic sense, but rather a temporary state where beings experience karmic consequences for their negative actions during their lifetimes. The duration of stay in Naraka is determined by the severity of these actions. Different traditions describe varying numbers of Naraka realms, each characterized by specific tortures and environments designed to purify the soul. These realms are governed by Yama, the lord of death, or other similar deities who oversee the dispensation of karmic justice. The ultimate goal is not eternal punishment, but rather the exhaustion of negative karma, allowing the being to be reborn into a higher realm. The descriptions of Naraka often serve as moral lessons, illustrating the consequences of unethical behavior and promoting virtuous conduct. The complexities of Naraka vary greatly between the different traditions that include this concept.
subtitle: A realm of suffering for karmic purification.
location: Located beneath the earth or in other cosmic dimensions.
significance: Naraka serves as a crucial component in the cycle of rebirth, acting as a temporary state of suffering for beings to exhaust negative karma and eventually be reborn into a higher realm. It reinforces the importance of ethical behavior and karmic consequences.

keyMyths.title: Yama's Judgement
keyMyths.description: The story often revolves around Yama, the lord of death, who judges souls based on their actions in life. He weighs their good and bad deeds, and accordingly, they are assigned to different realms within Naraka, each tailored to the specific nature of their transgressions. The intensity and duration of their suffering are directly proportional to the severity of their negative karma. After their time in Naraka, they are eventually reborn.

keyMyths.title: The Wheel of Suffering
keyMyths.description: In Buddhist cosmology, Naraka is depicted as one of the six realms of existence within the cycle of samsara. Beings are trapped in this wheel, constantly reborn into different realms based on their karma. Naraka is one of the lower realms, characterized by intense suffering and torment. Escaping Naraka requires cultivating virtue and wisdom to break free from the cycle of rebirth.

keyMyths.title: Naraka's Diverse Realms
keyMyths.description: The Naraka concept often includes many different realms or levels, each with distinct forms of torture and suffering. These realms are designed to address specific types of misdeeds, such as lying, stealing, or violence. The descriptions of these realms are often graphic and intended to deter individuals from engaging in harmful behavior. The suffering is not eternal, but a process of karmic cleansing.
keyMyths.source: Hindu Puranas, Buddhist Sutras, and Jain Agamas

sources.author: Wendy Doniger
sources.description: Provides a broad overview of Hindu beliefs, including the concept of Naraka and its role in the cycle of rebirth.
sources.text: Provides a broad overview of Hindu beliefs, including the concept of Naraka and its role in the cycle of rebirth.

sources.author: Jan Nattier
sources.work: A Few Good Men: The Bodhisattva Ideal in Theravada Buddhism
sources.description: Explores the Buddhist concept of Naraka in the context of karmic retribution and its impact on ethical behavior.
sources.text: Explores the Buddhist concept of Naraka in the context of karmic retribution and its impact on ethical behavior.

sources.author: Padmanabh S. Jaini
sources.description: Examines the Jain concept of Naraka as a realm of suffering resulting from negative karma and its role in the soul's journey.
sources.text: Examines the Jain concept of Naraka as a realm of suffering resulting from negative karma and its role in the soul's journey.
